lesson.name: Schopenhauer: The World as Will - Lesson 1 — The World as Will and Idea

sources.title: The World as Will and Idea

tasks.chapterTitle: The World as Idea: Epistemology and Sense Perception
Objectivity of the Will and Nature
The Platonic Idea and the Object of Art
The World as Will: Assertion and Denial of Life

messages.content:
Lesson 1 — The World as Will and Idea - Presentation25 Slides
The World Presents Two Aspects
  • The world stands before us as idea, that is, as object for a subject, yet this is only one side of its being.
  • When we consider the world from its other side we find that it is will, and this will proves to be simply that which the world is besides being idea.
Test your understanding of this lesson:

questions.question: According to the text, what is Kant’s thing-in-itself when reached by the way shown here?
questions.explanation: The excerpts state that Kant’s thing-in-itself is nothing but the will when the sphere of that conception is extended and defined.

questions.question: Why do the Ideas possess neither multiplicity nor change?
options: They lie outside the principle of sufficient reason
They are produced by the understanding
They belong to the principium individuationis
questions.explanation: The text says the Platonic Idea does not come under the principle of sufficient reason and therefore has neither multiplicity nor change.

questions.question: What does the text identify as the true being of matter?
questions.explanation: Matter is nothing more than causation; its true being is its action.

questions.question: How does music differ from the other arts in its relation to the will?
options: It is a direct copy of the will itself rather than of the Ideas
It copies only the Ideas of sound
It remains bound to the principle of sufficient reason
questions.explanation: Music is a direct objectification and copy of the whole will, passing over the Ideas.

questions.question: What change occurs in the will when the principium individuationis is lifted?
options: The will turns away from life in voluntary renunciation
The will asserts itself more strongly
The will becomes identical with reason
questions.explanation: With this knowledge the will turns away from life and attains perfect will-lessness.

cards.front: What single thought does the work impart according to the second book?
cards.back: That the world as idea is the objectification of will, which is the thing-in-itself.

cards.front: How does the text describe the relation of the Ideas to individual things?
cards.back: The Ideas are the eternal forms or prototypes; individuals are their copies that arise and pass away.

cards.front: Why can no cause be demanded for gravity or electricity?
cards.back: They are original forces, immediate manifestations of will, lying outside the chain of causes and effects.

cards.front: What does the text say music copies directly?
cards.back: Music is a direct copy of the will itself, not of the Ideas.

cards.front: Where is the thing-in-itself said to be present?
cards.back: Entire and undivided in every object of nature and in every living being.

cards.front: What enables man to know what he wills?
cards.back: Reason, which allows him to survey the connected series of his actions in the abstract.

cards.front: What marks the transition from virtue to asceticism?
cards.back: A horror of the will to live together with voluntary and complete chastity.

cards.front: Why do multiplicity and change belong only to the phenomenon?
cards.back: Because they are possible only through time, space and causality, which do not determine the thing-in-itself.

cards.front: What is matter according to the text?
cards.back: Nothing more than causation; its true being is its action.

cards.front: How does the will first receive knowledge of itself?
cards.back: Through the addition of the world as idea developed in subjection to it.

slides.title: The World Presents Two Aspects
bullets: The world stands before us as idea, that is, as object for a subject, yet this is only one side of its being.
When we consider the world from its other side we find that it is will, and this will proves to be simply that which the world is besides being idea.
In our own living bodies this second aspect becomes immediately present, since the very limbs and organs we see outwardly as objects are inwardly known as ceaseless striving and impulse.

The consequence is that every force of nature and every living being must be understood as the same will objectified in countless degrees, so that the whole of reality is bound together by one underlying drive rather than by any rational plan.
slides.notes: Sets the fundamental distinction drawn in the second book.

slides.title: Kant’s Thing-in-Itself Remains Obscure
bullets: Kant reached the thing-in-itself by an inference from the caused to the cause, leaving it a stumbling-stone in his system.
The doctrine appears significant yet remains obscure and paradoxical because it is introduced merely as an unknown X.

Because the thing-in-itself is reached only by an empty causal inference and then left nameless, the critical philosophy ends by declaring the true nature of reality forever inaccessible, so that metaphysics itself appears to halt at an unbridgeable frontier.

This purely negative result creates the further difficulty that no account can be given of how the unknown X is supposed to affect us at all, leaving the entire doctrine suspended between a necessary assumption and an absolute refusal to say anything positive about what has been assumed.
slides.notes: Prepares the contrast with Schopenhauer’s direct identification.

slides.title: The Will Is the Thing-in-Itself
bullets: If reached by the entirely different way shown here, Kant’s thing-in-itself is nothing but the will when the sphere of that conception is extended and defined.
The will therefore supplies the inner reality that Kant could only indicate negatively.

Once the will is recognized as the hidden kernel of every phenomenon, the entire spectacle of nature and history appears as the ceaseless objectification of a single, striving reality rather than a set of unknowable causes.

This identification carries the further consequence that the intellect, far from being an independent knower, arises only as an instrument of the will and therefore remains subordinate to the very force it tries to comprehend.
slides.notes: Core identification of the lesson.

slides.title: A Verbatim Statement of the Identification
bullets: Kant says time, space and causality belong only to the phenomenon; therefore the thing-in-itself lies outside these forms.

What Plato calls the eternal Ideas and what Kant calls the thing-in-itself are thereby brought into close relation through the single circumstance that both are the will at definite grades of its objectification.

Once the thing-in-itself is freed from every form of appearance, our own inner experience of willing becomes the only immediate path into that hidden region, letting us feel directly what otherwise remains merely inferred.

The result is that every natural force, every living drive, and every human motive now counts as one and the same reality viewed at successive levels of visibility, so the apparent variety of the world collapses into a single, blind striving.
slides.notes: Contains the explicit linkage of the two great paradoxes.

slides.title: Objectification Occurs in Definite Grades
bullets: The objectification of will takes place in many definite grades in which the nature of will appears with gradually increasing distinctness.
These grades are the determined species, the original unchanging forms and qualities of all natural bodies and forces.
At the lowest grades the will therefore shows itself only as an unconscious drive within stone or plant, so that its inner nature remains almost entirely veiled from any observer.

Yet precisely because the grades ascend without altering the will itself, the human intellect at the highest grade can finally turn its gaze inward and recognize that same striving as the hidden reality of every phenomenon.
slides.notes: Introduces the hierarchy of objectification.

slides.title: The Grades Are Plato’s Ideas
bullets: The definite grades of the objectification of will are simply Plato’s Ideas, the eternal forms that do not enter time and space.
They remain fixed, subject to no change, always being, while particular things arise and pass away.

Through these unchanging Ideas the will first steps forth from its numinous unity into a visible order of distinct characters, each one a complete expression of striving that no individual specimen can ever exhaust.

The endless coming-to-be and perishing of single things therefore serves merely as the temporal theater in which these eternal grades are repeatedly enacted, leaving the underlying will itself untouched by generation or decay.
slides.notes: Equates the grades with the Ideas.

slides.title: Ideas Stand Outside the Principle of Sufficient Reason
bullets: The Platonic Idea does not come under the principle of sufficient reason and therefore possesses neither multiplicity nor change.
While individuals in which it expresses itself are innumerable, the Idea itself remains one and the same.

Because the Idea stands free of space time and causality it lets the intellect behold the will itself as the single inner essence that underlies all phenomena rather than any fleeting individual instance.

This timeless unity therefore creates the decisive tension that aesthetic contemplation can grasp the will in its pure objectification while everyday perception remains trapped among the countless changing copies.
slides.notes: Explains why Ideas escape the forms of the phenomenon.

slides.title: The Lowest Grades Appear in Universal Forces
bullets: The lowest grades of the objectification of will are the most universal forces of nature such as gravity and impenetrability.
These forces are immediate manifestations of will, groundless like human character, and lie outside the chain of causes and effects.
In these forces the will therefore stands naked before us, stripped of every veil of individuality or motive, so that any attempt to ask why gravity pulls or matter resists must end in silence.

Their groundlessness thus reveals a deeper consequence: the entire causal order of nature rests upon something that itself lies beyond cause and effect, leaving scientific explanation forever incomplete at its foundation.
slides.notes: Begins the ascent through the grades.

slides.title: Matter Is Causality and Therefore Will
bullets: Matter is nothing more than causation; its true being is its action, and only as active does it fill space and time.
Whoever has recognised the law of causation has completely mastered the nature of matter as such.

Once matter stands revealed as nothing but the chain of effects, every natural force appears as the visible ripple of an inner drive that presses outward without ever being exhausted by its own expressions.

The consequence is that the ultimate ground of all phenomena cannot itself be another phenomenon; it must be the will, whose ceaseless striving alone explains why causal activity fills and sustains the world we perceive.
slides.notes: Shows the identity of matter with the lowest objectification.

slides.title: The Force Itself Is Not an Effect
bullets: It is senseless to demand a cause of gravity or electricity, for they are original forces whose particular manifestations alone are subordinated to the principle of sufficient reason.
The force lies outside time and is the prior condition of all causes and effects.
Once we recognize that an original force needs no antecedent, the same logic shows why the will cannot be derived from anything else without destroying the very notion of ground and consequence.
This places the will outside every explanatory sequence, so that all natural events become its temporal unfolding rather than its hidden causes.
slides.notes: Clarifies the groundlessness of natural forces.

slides.title: The Thing-in-Itself Is Present Entire in Every Object
bullets: The thing-in-itself whose manifestation is the world cannot have its true self spread out and dispersed in boundless space.
It is present entire and undivided in every object of nature and in every living being.

Because the will stands wholly inside each single object, the innermost nature of even the smallest stone or fleeting insect already holds everything that could ever be discovered about reality itself.

This undivided presence turns the endless variety of phenomena into a mere surface play, so that the true unity of the world is not lost among countless separate things but remains equally near at every point we choose to look.
slides.notes: Emphasises the non-spatial character of the will.

slides.title: True Wisdom Does Not Require Measuring the Universe
bullets: When we consider the vastness of the world the most important point is that endless extension belongs only to the manifestation, not to the thing-in-itself.
True wisdom is attained by the thorough investigation of any individual thing rather than by traversing endless space.

Because the will is wholly present in every finite appearance, the thinker who penetrates the inner nature of even one living thing already stands face to face with the single reality that underlies all appearances.
Consequently the mind that still demands ever wider surveys of space merely prolongs its own distraction and postpones the decisive turn from outer multiplicity to the inner identity of the will.
slides.notes: Draws the practical consequence of the doctrine.

slides.title: The Will Receives Knowledge Through the Idea
bullets: The will, considered purely in itself, is without knowledge and is merely a blind incessant impulse.
Through the addition of the world as idea the will receives the knowledge of its own willing and of what it is that it wills.
In this way the idea functions as an inner mirror, letting the will first recognize its own ceaseless pressure as directed toward definite objects rather than remaining an aimless surge.
The same illumination, however, binds the will to the forms of space, time, and causality, so that every striving now appears as a calculated pursuit whose satisfaction is endlessly postponed.
slides.notes: Shows how the two aspects unite in man.

slides.title: The Highest Grade of Objectification Is Man
bullets: The ascending grades of distinctness reach their highest point in man, whose nature receives its complete expression only through the whole connected series of his actions.
Reason enables man to survey this series in the abstract and thereby to know what he wills.
Through this unbroken chain of actions man therefore stands as the clearest mirror in which the will beholds its own character, no longer hidden in plant or beast but displayed in full sequence.
Reason’s abstract glance consequently lets him measure the endlessness of that striving and sense, already in life, the quiet judgment that may one day turn against the will itself.
slides.notes: Locates humanity within the hierarchy.

slides.title: The Will Is a Blind Incessant Impulse
bullets: In unorganised and vegetable nature the will appears as a blind incessant impulse without knowledge.
The same blind impulse constitutes the vegetative part of our own life.

This same blind striving therefore precedes every form of consciousness and continues its work whether awareness is present or absent, so that the greater part of nature and of our own bodily existence unfolds without ever consulting knowledge.
As a result the intellect appears only as a late and partial servant, forever trying to steer or interpret drives whose origin and persistence lie far beyond its reach.
slides.notes: Describes the character of will prior to knowledge.

slides.title: The World as Idea Is the Mirror of the Will
bullets: The first three books convey that the world as idea is the complete mirror of the will in which it knows itself in ascending grades of distinctness.
The highest of these grades is man, yet even man requires the whole connected series of actions to express the will fully.

Through this ascending series of objectifications the will therefore beholds its own inner character as blind striving, yet the beholding itself occurs only within the sphere of representation and leaves the will untouched in its noumenal reality.

The result is a permanent tension: every human life, however fully unfolded across its deeds, still functions as one more finite mirror that can never capture the will’s endless essence, so the intellect remains an instrument forever external to what it reveals.
slides.notes: Summarises the mirror relation.

slides.title: The Principle of Sufficient Reason Governs Only the Phenomenon
bullets: Time, space and causality are the forms under which the subject knows as an individual; they therefore belong only to the phenomenon.
The thing-in-itself, the will, lies outside these forms and is not subject to them.
Because these forms never reach the will itself, no chain of causes can ever account for why the will exists or strives as it does.
The thinker who demands a sufficient reason for existence therefore mistakes the veil for the thing behind it and remains forever unsatisfied.
slides.notes: Recalls the Kantian limit now filled by will.

slides.title: Multiplicity Belongs Only to the Phenomenon
bullets: All multiplicity and all coming into being and passing away are possible only through time, space and causality.
Consequently multiplicity and change belong only to the phenomenon, not to the thing-in-itself.

Because the will stands outside space and time it cannot split into separate centers of striving; whatever appears as many distinct wills is only the one will encountering itself through the forms of individuation.

The practical consequence is that every act of violence or compassion ultimately touches the same underlying reality, since the boundaries that seem to separate one being from another have no purchase on the will itself.
slides.notes: Explains why the will itself is one.

slides.title: The Ideas Are the Adequate Objectivity of Will
bullets: The Ideas are the adequate objectification of will at definite grades, each Idea being one and unchanging.
The multiplied manifestation of these Ideas in time and space constitutes the visible world of individual things.

Because each Idea fixes the Will at one unchanging grade, the endless birth and death of individuals never alters the essential character of any species, so that nature repeats the same inner conflict at every moment without ever advancing beyond it.

This repetition shows that the thing-in-itself remains hidden behind the veil of multiplicity, and only by turning from the transient copies toward the single, eternal Idea can we grasp the Will in the form that truly belongs to it.
slides.notes: States the precise relation of Ideas to will.

slides.title: Music Copies the Will Directly
bullets: Music is a direct objectification and copy of the whole will, just as the world itself is, and even as the Ideas are.
Because it passes over the Ideas, music is entirely independent of the phenomenal world and could to a certain extent exist if there were no world at all.
This direct route means that music can stir the listener with the raw force of endless striving and fleeting respite, without ever having to name a particular desire or object.
One consequence is that music alone among the arts can reach the will as thing-in-itself, granting us an immediate sense of existence that bypasses every veil of representation.
slides.notes: Shows an art that bypasses Ideas to reach will itself.

slides.title: The Effect of Music Reveals Its Relation to the Will
bullets: The effect of music is so much more powerful and penetrating than that of the other arts because they speak only of shadows while music speaks of the thing itself.
Since the same will objectifies itself both in the Ideas and in music, though in different ways, a deep analogy must exist between them.

Thus music reaches straight into the listener's own striving and makes that striving audible to itself, so that the hearer experiences the restless surge of existence without needing any particular scene or story to provoke it.

The analogy therefore implies that every melodic line and harmonic shift corresponds to some fundamental movement of the will, allowing us to recognize our own inner conflicts and resolutions in purely tonal form and thereby to feel both understood and inescapably bound to the life-will at once.
slides.notes: Uses aesthetic evidence to confirm the metaphysics.

slides.title: The Will Turns Away When the Veil Is Lifted
bullets: When the principium individuationis is lifted to a high degree a man recognises the identity of will in all manifestations and regards the suffering of all beings as his own.
With this knowledge the will turns away from life, shudders at its pleasures, and attains voluntary renunciation and perfect will-lessness.
The man who has pierced the veil therefore finds every personal triumph hollow, since the same striving that once promised satisfaction now reveals itself as the shared torment of all creatures.

Such knowledge does not merely soften the heart but dissolves the very impulse to assert oneself against others, leaving only the quiet resolve to cease adding to the sum of suffering that the will itself has created.
slides.notes: Indicates the ethical consequence of knowing the will.

slides.title: Asceticism Is the Phenomenon of Denial
bullets: The transition from virtue to asceticism is marked by a horror of the nature of which one’s own phenomenal existence is an expression.
The ascetic denies the will and gives the lie to the body, seeking the greatest indifference to everything.

Once this horror takes hold, the individual no longer seeks merely to alleviate the suffering of others but turns against the will itself, so that every bodily impulse becomes an occasion for refusal rather than satisfaction.
The result is a lived contradiction: the same will that sustains the body is now starved by it, producing a freedom from the tyranny of desire that no mere moral virtue could ever secure.
slides.notes: Describes the visible sign of the denial of the will.
